Setup

You will be interacting with code a bit differently with this assignment. We are introducing several new packages and functions this week. With so many new functions and workflows, it makes more sense to have you interact with the code in a more guided format. Use this template to complete this assignment.

New concepts introduced

  • Chaining and new workflows for data wrangling with dplyr
  • Better, more complex visualization with ggplot
  • Pivoting with tidyr and merging data with dplyr

[Delete these and any other unecessary instructions for your submission - Keep it clean]


Load packages and datesets

R Markdown documents must be self-contained, meaning all code should run successfully within the document itself. As such, you need to load all necessary packages and datasets for output to be generated (this includes correct filepaths based on the location of the document).

# load packages
# install.packages("dplyr") - must install packages once on your machine!
library(dplyr) # must load packages in every session
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)


Read in the “UTSNTL_ALL_2020_2022_Daily_Wide.csv” dataset. This file contains meteorological and snow data from snow telemetry (snotel) weather stations in the mountains of Utah. Data from three winter seasons in 2020-21, 2021-22, and 2022-23 were added to this dataset (water years 2021-23). Several different variables are included for each station:

# replace the filepath based on your folder organization
data_path <- "../data/UTSNTL_ALL_2020_2022_Daily_Wide.csv" # modify PATH!
df_snotel <- read.csv(data_path)
df_snotel[1:2, 1:3] # print first two rows and first two columns
##      Date Agua.Canyon..907..Snow.Water.Equivalent..in..Start.of.Day.Values
## 1 10/1/20                                                                0
## 2 10/2/20                                                                0
##   Agua.Canyon..907..Snow.Depth..in..Start.of.Day.Values
## 1                                                     0
## 2                                                     0

You might realize that this data set is in a strange format:

cat("The raw snotel data is in wide format \n with", nrow(df_snotel),
    "days (rows) across 3 water years and", ncol(df_snotel),
    "variables (columns) \n \nThe first 10 column names reveal the structure:\n",
    paste0(colnames(df_snotel)[1:10], "\n") )
## The raw snotel data is in wide format 
##  with 1095 days (rows) across 3 water years and 823 variables (columns) 
##  
## The first 10 column names reveal the structure:
##  Date
##  Agua.Canyon..907..Snow.Water.Equivalent..in..Start.of.Day.Values
##  Agua.Canyon..907..Snow.Depth..in..Start.of.Day.Values
##  Agua.Canyon..907..Precipitation.Accumulation..in..Start.of.Day.Values
##  Agua.Canyon..907..Air.Temperature.Average..degF.
##  Agua.Canyon..907..Air.Temperature.Observed..degF..Start.of.Day.Values
##  Agua.Canyon..907..Air.Temperature.Maximum..degF.
##  Atwater..1308..Snow.Water.Equivalent..in..Start.of.Day.Values
##  Atwater..1308..Snow.Depth..in..Start.of.Day.Values
##  Atwater..1308..Precipitation.Accumulation..in..Start.of.Day.Values

As you can see, the column names contain information about the station name (e.g., Agua Canyon, Atwater, etc.), station ID, variable name (e.g., Snow water equivalent, Snow Depth, etc.), units (e.g., inches, degF, etc.), and how daily value was extracted (Start of Day). Essentially, there are 3 years of data, with 6 variables, making a total of 137 stations.

Wide format, like this, is one way that you might find or download data. It can be difficult to understand your data in a mixed format like this. You will use new packages and functions to visualize and transform this data.

Modify the code provided below to help answer questions and generate results


Part 1. Chaining and visualizing

Let’s start with %>% (chaining in dplyr) and visualization in ggplot. These workflows allow you to group functions together to subset data and the ggplot functions provide greater flexibility for better looking plots. It is a new workflow to get used to, but very useful!


Question 1
  1. Modify the script below to show the the average and max air temperature over three seasons Agua Canyon snotel station (Note <...> mean you need to add something). Read annotations carefully
df_snotel %>% # this is "Chaining" it connects output to the next function
  # modify date format
  mutate(Date=as.Date(Date,format="%m/%d/%y")) %>% # chain
  # date column is x-axis - use mapping aesthetic or "aes"
  ggplot(aes(x=Date)) + # ggplot objects linked with "+"
  # add y-axis line for Agua Canyon air temperature average - use "aes"
  geom_line(aes(y = Agua.Canyon..907..Air.Temperature.Average..degF., 
                color="Average"), linewidth=0.5) +
  # Add max air temperature
  geom_line(aes(y = Agua.Canyon..907..Air.Temperature.Maximum..degF., 
                color="Maximum"), linewidth=0.5) +
  # manually set colors - these map to the keys input in the line functions above
  scale_color_manual(
    name = "Temperature",
    values = c("Average" = "orange",
               "Maximum" = "firebrick")
  ) +
  # provide labels
  labs(title = "Agua Canyon Snotel (Water years 2021-2023)",
       y = "Air temperature (ºF)",
       x = "") +
  theme_bw() # add a nice looking theme


Question 2
  1. Modify the following code to visualize both SWE and Average Temperature for Agua Canyon over the three years. This requires adding a second axis (right side) and scaling values so they can be observed side-by-side.
# value to scale data (see where this is used below)
scale_swe <- 3 # important in visualizing two different data ranges side-by-side 

# start plot
df_snotel %>%
  mutate(Date=as.Date(Date,format="%m/%d/%y")) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x=Date)) + 
  # Add SWE first to be behind line
  geom_bar(aes(y = Agua.Canyon..907..Snow.Water.Equivalent..in..Start.of.Day.Values), 
           color='lightblue', stat='identity') +
  # add average temperature
  geom_line(aes(y = Agua.Canyon..907..Air.Temperature.Average..degF. /scale_swe), 
            color="firebrick", linewidth = 0.5) +
  # label the y-axis and add a second axis on the right for the temperature, scale values as above
  scale_y_continuous(name = "SWE (inches)", sec.axis = sec_axis( transform = ~.* scale_swe, name= "Temperature (ºF)")) +
  labs(title = "Agua Canyon Snotel (Water years 2021-2023)") +
  theme_classic() # add a nice looking theme


Part 2. Pivoting and summarizing

This section will require you to transform the data through pivoting, filtering, and subsetting.

Modify the code below each question to produce the requested results.


Question 3
  1. Select all snow depth columns and pivot the data to a long format to show each station in a column. Remove all 0 values to exclude the non-winter season (i.e. summer). Group by station then use the summarize function to show the mean snow depth for each station. Show the `head()
df_snotel %>% mutate(Date=as.Date(Date,format="%m/%d/%y")) %>%
  # select all columns that "contain" a pattern (how does snow depth appear?)
  select(Date, contains("Snow.Depth")) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols=!Date, # don't pivot date but do the other values
               names_to = "station", values_to = "depth") %>%
  # clean up station names (keep everything before first "..")
  mutate(station = sub("\\.\\..*$", "", station)) %>% 
  # replace "." with space
  mutate(station = gsub("\\.", " ", station)) %>% 
  # filter for non-zero values
  filter(depth > 0) %>%
  # group by value
  group_by(station) %>% 
  # summarize
  summarize(station_mean = mean(depth)) %>% 
  head()
## # A tibble: 6 × 2
##   station         station_mean
##   <chr>                  <dbl>
## 1 Agua Canyon             20.0
## 2 Atwater                100. 
## 3 Bear River RS           20.9
## 4 Beaver Dams             20.4
## 5 Beaver Divide           28.8
## 6 Ben Lomond Peak         53.7


Question 4
  1. Use the first part of the code in the previous question to pivot and filter the data to depth values greater than 0. Instead of grouping and summarizing as you did previously, use mutate to create a new field that indicates the water year. Then, show only the top five snotel sites with the deepest snow depth for each winter season. Include the max snow depth value (inches) and the Date of max snow depth for each season. Show the top fire sites for each season and describe when the date of max snow depth occurs.
# code
df_snotel %>%  mutate(Date=as.Date(Date,format="%m/%d/%y")) %>%
  select(Date, contains("Snow.Depth")) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols=!Date,
               names_to = "station", values_to = "depth") %>%
  filter(depth>0) %>%
  # generate water year from dates
  mutate(water_year = cut(Date, breaks =  c(as.Date(c("2020-10-01", "2021-10-01", "2022-10-01")), Inf), 
                      labels = paste0("WY_", c(2021, 2022, 2023)))) %>%
  mutate(station = gsub("\\.", " ", sub("\\.\\..*$", "", station))) %>% 
  group_by(station, water_year) %>%
  summarise(snow_max = max(depth, na.rm=T),
            max_day = Date[which.max(depth)],
            .groups = "drop") %>%
  group_by(water_year) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = snow_max, n = 5)
## # A tibble: 16 × 4
## # Groups:   water_year [3]
##    station         water_year snow_max max_day   
##    <chr>           <fct>         <int> <date>    
##  1 Snowbird        WY_2021          95 2021-02-18
##  2 Tony Grove Lake WY_2021          90 2021-02-28
##  3 Ben Lomond Peak WY_2021          81 2021-02-18
##  4 Lookout Peak    WY_2021          78 2021-02-18
##  5 Farmington      WY_2021          76 2021-02-28
##  6 Tony Grove Lake WY_2022          86 2022-01-07
##  7 Snowbird        WY_2022          83 2022-03-10
##  8 Usu Doc Daniel  WY_2022          79 2022-04-16
##  9 Farmington      WY_2022          72 2022-03-10
## 10 Big Flat        WY_2022          71 2022-01-01
## 11 Mt Baldy        WY_2022          71 2022-01-01
## 12 Atwater         WY_2023         229 2023-04-06
## 13 Ben Lomond Peak WY_2023         205 2023-04-05
## 14 Farmington      WY_2023         201 2023-04-05
## 15 Snowbird        WY_2023         199 2023-04-05
## 16 Parrish Creek   WY_2023         165 2023-04-05

Add a statement


Question 5
  1. Use the results above to generate a bar plot in ggplot showing the counts of snotel sites in the top five for for snow depth over the three seasons. Which sites continually receive the deepest snow depth?
df_snotel %>%  mutate(Date=as.Date(Date,format="%m/%d/%y")) %>%
  select(Date, contains("Snow.Depth")) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols=!Date,
               names_to = "station", values_to = "depth") %>%
  filter(depth>0) %>%
  # generate water year from dates
  mutate(water_year = cut(Date, breaks =  c(as.Date(c("2020-10-01", "2021-10-01", "2022-10-01")), Inf), 
                      labels = paste0("WY_", c(2021, 2022, 2023)))) %>%
  mutate(station = gsub("\\.", " ", sub("\\.\\..*$", "", station))) %>% 
  group_by(station, water_year) %>%
  summarise(snow_max = max(depth, na.rm=T),
            max_day = Date[which.max(depth)], 
            .groups = "drop") %>%
  group_by(water_year) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = snow_max, n = 5) %>% 
  select(station) %>% 
  # bar plot showing station counts
  ggplot(aes(x=station)) + 
  geom_bar(stat = "count", fill = "lightblue") +
  labs(
      title="Stations with deepest snow in UT WY 21-23",
      x = "", y = "Times in top 5 season deepest")  +
    theme( # change x label angle to 45 degrees
      axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
    )
## Adding missing grouping variables: `water_year`


Question 6
  1. Use parts of your scripts above to show the average snow water equivalence (SWE) by month for all stations. Create a bar plot showing average SWE by month using the code below and describe when max swe typically occurs.
df_snotel %>%  mutate(Date=as.Date(Date,format="%m/%d/%y")) %>%
  select(Date, contains("Snow.Water")) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols=!Date,
               names_to = "station", values_to = "swe") %>%
  filter(swe>0) %>%
  # generate water year from dates
  mutate(month = format(Date, "%m")) %>%
  # mutate(station = gsub("\\.", " ", sub("\\.\\..*$", "", station))) %>% 
  group_by(month) %>%
  summarise(swe_mean = mean(swe, na.rm=T),
            .groups = "drop") %>%
  # bar plot showing station counts
  ggplot(aes(x=month, y = swe_mean)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "lightblue") +
  labs(
      title="Monthly SWE, UT (WY21-23)",
      x = "", y = "SWE average (in)")  +
    theme_classic()


Question 7
  1. Combine several steps used above with other dplyr and ggplot workflows to show monthly snow depth and SWE for each water year (organized by water year month). Leverage the aesthetic mapping in ggplot to do this in a single plot.
df_snotel %>% 
  mutate(Date=as.Date(Date,format="%m/%d/%y")) %>% 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = !Date,
    names_to = c("station", "variable"),
    names_pattern = "^(.*?)\\.\\.[0-9]+\\.\\.(.*?)\\.\\..*$",
    values_to = "values") %>%
  mutate(station = gsub("\\.", " ", station)) %>% 
  mutate(variable = gsub("\\.", " ", variable)) %>% 
  # generate water year from dates
  mutate(water_year = cut(Date, breaks =  c(as.Date(c("2020-10-01", "2021-10-01", "2022-10-01")), Inf), 
                      labels = paste("Water year", c(2021, 2022, 2023)))) %>%
  # only keep snow depth and swe
  filter( grepl("Snow", variable) ) %>%
  filter(values > 0) %>%
  # generate water year from dates
  mutate(month = format(Date, "%m")) %>%
  mutate(
    month = month.abb[as.numeric(month)],
    month = factor(month, levels = month.abb[c(10:12, 1:9)])
  ) %>% 
  group_by(water_year, month, variable) %>%
  summarise(var_month = mean(values, na.rm=T),
            .groups = "drop") %>%
  # take monthly averages
  # plot both side-by-side
  ggplot(aes(x = month, y = var_month, fill=variable)) +
  geom_bar(stat='identity') +
  facet_wrap(.~water_year, scales = "free_x") +
  labs(
      title="Monthly snow depth and water equivalence, UT",
      x = "", y = "Depth (in)")  +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    legend.position = c(0.15, 0.75),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1)
  )
## Warning: A numeric `legend.position` argument in `theme()` was deprecated in ggplot2
## 3.5.0.
## ℹ Please use the `legend.position.inside` argument of `theme()` instead.
## This warning is displayed once every 8 hours.
## Call `lifecycle::last_lifecycle_warnings()` to see where this warning was
## generated.


Part 3. Joining datasets

Snow water equivalent (SWE) is an important metric used to assess the amount of water in the snowpack. In particular, as you have seen, the month of April can be critical for water managers to assess conditions for the coming year.

Create a new summary table using the snotel data from above, to show the max SWE amount for the month of April during season 2.

Read in the “UTSNTL_ELEV.csv” file and perform a join (e.g. left_join) with your summarized table of max april SWE. Be sure to make any necessay adjustments to the site names between datasets as they are slightly different.

modify the file path as needed

df_elev <- read.csv("../data/UTSNTL_ELEV.csv")
head(df_elev)
##                names elevation_ft
## 1      Bear River RS         8777
## 2 Burts Miller Ranch         8000
## 3        Hayden Fork         9130
## 4          Lily Lake         9133
## 5       Monte Cristo         8932
## 6     Dry Bread Pond         8302

Clean up some of these names by removing punctuation. This will allow us to join data more easily.

# run the following code
df_elev <- df_elev %>% 
  # remove punctuation
  mutate(names = gsub("[[:punct:]]", " ", names)) %>% 
  # remove any double space
  mutate(names = sub("  .*", "", names)) %>% 
  # remove any remaining whitespace
  mutate(names = trimws(names))


Question 8
  1. Create a new dataset from the snotel data showing max April SWE from WY 2022, and join this with the elevation dataframe. Show the distributions of SWE and elevation as side-by-side histograms
df_snotel %>% mutate(Date=as.Date(Date,format="%m/%d/%y")) %>%
  select(Date, contains("Snow.Water")) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols=!Date,
               names_to = "station", values_to = "swe") %>%
  filter(swe > 0) %>%
  mutate(water_year = cut(Date, breaks =  c(as.Date(c("2020-10-01", "2021-10-01", "2022-10-01")), Inf), 
                      labels = paste0("WY_", c(2021, 2022, 2023)))) %>%
  mutate(station = gsub("\\.", " ", sub("\\.\\..*$", "", station))) %>% 
  # select only dates in april for WY 2022
  mutate(month = (format(Date, "%m"))) %>% 
  filter(month=="04") %>% 
  filter(water_year == "WY_2022") %>% 
  group_by(station) %>%
  summarise(swe_max = max(swe, na.rm=T),
            .groups = "drop") %>% 
  left_join(df_elev, by=c("station" = "names")) 
## # A tibble: 133 × 3
##    station          swe_max elevation_ft
##    <chr>              <dbl>        <int>
##  1 Agua Canyon          3.6         8900
##  2 Bear River RS        7.3         8777
##  3 Beaver Dams          6.6         7990
##  4 Beaver Divide        9.5         8280
##  5 Ben Lomond Peak     16.4         7689
##  6 Ben Lomond Trail     6.3         5972
##  7 Bevans Cabin         1           6520
##  8 Big Flat            19.9        10349
##  9 Black Flat U M       6.3         9414
## 10 Blacks Fork Jct      7.6         8869
## # ℹ 123 more rows


Question 9
  1. Use your joined dataset to create a scatter plot that shows the max SWE (snow water equivalent) during the month of April as a function of elevation (x-axis). Plot ussing ggplot and add a trendline
# Use the dataset above to 
df_snotel %>% mutate(Date=as.Date(Date,format="%m/%d/%y")) %>%
  select(Date, contains("Snow.Water")) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols=!Date,
               names_to = "station", values_to = "swe") %>%
  filter(swe > 0) %>%
  mutate(water_year = cut(Date, breaks =  c(as.Date(c("2020-10-01", "2021-10-01", "2022-10-01")), Inf), 
                      labels = paste0("WY_", c(2021, 2022, 2023)))) %>%
  mutate(station = gsub("\\.", " ", sub("\\.\\..*$", "", station))) %>% 
  # select only dates in april for WY 2022
  mutate(month = (format(Date, "%m"))) %>% 
  filter(month=="04") %>% 
  filter(water_year == "WY_2022") %>% 
  group_by(station) %>%
  summarise(swe_max = max(swe, na.rm=T),
            max_day = Date[which.max(swe)],
            .groups = "drop") %>% 
  left_join(df_elev, by=c("station" = "names")) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x=elevation_ft, y = swe_max)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_bw()


Question 10
  1. It is well known that precipitation amounts can be enhanced at higher elevations due to orographic lift (air masses flowing over mountains). Utah’s mountains benefit and often have an enhancement of 5+ times the amount of precipitation that the valleys receive. Statistically assess the strength of the linear relationship between April SWE as a function of elevation. Consider the direction, strength, significance, and wellness of fit of this relationship, and discuss whether elevation is a good predictor of SWE.
# use functions previously covered to assess the relationship

Discuss whether this is a good predictor of SWE. Hypothesize as to why.
